title: W10: Vision-Language and Diffusion Models
blurb: Medium priority from the available exams, but important because the syllabus explicitly mentions CLIP and diffusion. Diffusion appears directly in Summer 2025.

knowledge_points.title: Denoising Diffusion Models
knowledge_points.recap: Diffusion models have a forward noising process and a learned reverse denoising process.

questions.q: Describe the forward and reverse processes in a denoising diffusion model.
questions.answer: The forward process gradually adds noise to the data. The reverse process learns to generate data by iteratively denoising, moving from noise back toward a data sample.
questions.extend: This is short but very likely to be asked as a concept definition.

questions.q: Why does diffusion generation require multiple inference steps?
questions.answer: The model usually removes noise gradually over a sequence of denoising steps. More steps increase inference-time compute but can improve sample quality, depending on the sampler.

knowledge_points.title: CLIP and Vision-Language Models
knowledge_points.recap: The provided exams do not directly ask CLIP, but the syllabus names vision-language models. Know the high-level contrastive idea and how image/text embeddings are aligned.

questions.q: What is the core training idea behind CLIP-style vision-language models?
questions.answer: CLIP trains an image encoder and a text encoder so matching image-text pairs have similar embeddings and mismatched pairs are far apart. At inference, labels or prompts can be embedded as text and compared to image embeddings for zero-shot classification.
questions.extend: This is syllabus-driven practice, not extracted from the three provided FoFM exams.

questions.q: Why are CLIP-style models useful for zero-shot recognition?
questions.answer: Because class names or natural-language prompts can be represented in the same embedding space as images. The model can compare an image embedding to text prompt embeddings even for categories not used as fixed classifier heads during supervised training.
